questions.question: The verb “give” can take two objects: “They gave {a1} a prize.”
Active: “They gave Mary a prize.” Passive: “Mary {a1} a prize.”
Active: “They sent him a letter.” Passive: “A letter {a1} him.”
In “She was offered a job,” the indirect object becomes the {a1}.
Use “to” in “The keys were handed {a1} the manager.”
“A new laptop was bought {a1} him” keeps the receiver clear.
For people, “He was told the truth” is usually more natural than “The truth was told {a1} him.”
Active: “The guide showed us the room.” Passive: “We {a1} the room.”
Do not write “A book was given him” in standard learner English; write “A book was given {a1} him.”
“Teach” is a two-object verb in “They taught {a1} grammar.”
“A medal was awarded {a1} the winner” uses the thing as subject.
“The winner {a1} a medal” uses the receiver as subject.
